name: QBE
summary: Quentin Carbonneaux's compact compiler backend in ~14,000 lines of C99 with zero dependencies. Delivers ~50–70% of LLVM -O2 performance at near-instant compile times. SSA-based IL, modest optimisation set, full C ABI on amd64/arm64/riscv64. The cproc C11 compiler is the canonical consumer.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
QBE is a compiler backend by Quentin Carbonneaux that targets the sweet spot between TCC (no optimisation) and LLVM (full industrial optimiser). Written in ~14,000 lines of C99 with zero dependencies, QBE provides:

- Uniform SSA-based IL used at all compilation stages
- Copy elimination, sparse conditional constant propagation, dead instruction elimination
- Registerisation of small stack slots
- Linear register allocator with hinting (split spiller + allocator, enabled by SSA)
- Full C ABI support on amd64, arm64, and riscv64

Benchmarks show QBE-compiled code runs roughly 50–70% the speed of LLVM -O2. Compilation is near-instant (~2 seconds for the compiler itself with `-O2`).

sections.title: Design philosophy and use cases
sections.content:
The `cproc` C11 compiler (8,000 lines of C) uses QBE as its backend and can build GCC 4.7, binutils, git, and much more. The combination of cproc + QBE is roughly 22,000 lines total — a complete C11 compiler and code generator that produces 50–70% of GCC's performance.

The lesson for new languages: you don't need LLVM to get reasonable codegen. QBE provides a self-contained, hackable, and understandable backend that any language can target. For bootstrapping compilers or interpreter-to-native tiers — where small dependency footprint and hackability matter more than peak code quality — QBE is one of the strongest options to evaluate.
